slides.title: Mahalanobis Distance for Robust Classification
slides.notes: Introduces the core method: using Mahalanobis distance to improve classification robustness, especially for detecting out-of-distribution samples.

steps.heading: Problem Motivation
steps.content: Traditional classifiers like Softmax struggle with out-of-distribution (OOD) data. This paper proposes Mahalanobis distance-based methods to detect and handle such cases.

steps.heading: Key Insight
steps.content: Mahalanobis distance measures how far a sample is from class means in feature space, accounting for covariance structure for better OOD detection.

steps.heading: Method Overview
steps.content: The paper introduces two main algorithms: one for confidence scoring and another for incremental learning, both leveraging Mahalanobis distance.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: This approach improves accuracy and robustness on datasets like CIFAR-10, SVHN, and TinyImageNet, especially under adversarial conditions.

slides.title: Figure 1: Performance of Mahalanobis vs Softmax
slides.subtitle: ResNet Results
slides.notes: This figure demonstrates that Mahalanobis distance improves classification accuracy and OOD detection compared to Softmax.

steps.heading: Visualization (a)
steps.content: t-SNE plot shows clusters of features from different classes. Mahalanobis distance helps separate these clusters more distinctly than Softmax.

steps.heading: Accuracy (b)
steps.content: Bar chart shows Mahalanobis achieves higher test accuracy across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN datasets.

steps.heading: ROC Curve (c)
steps.content: ROC curves indicate Mahalanobis has superior OOD detection capability, as shown by higher TPR at lower FPR compared to Softmax and Euclidean.

steps.heading: Overall Impact
steps.content: Mahalanobis distance enhances both in-distribution accuracy and OOD detection performance significantly over traditional methods.

slides.title: Figure 2: AUC Comparison Across Datasets
slides.subtitle: DenseNet Confidence Scoring
slides.notes: Shows the effectiveness of the proposed confidence score across different datasets and attack types.

steps.heading: Dataset Performance
steps.content: AUROC scores are high across all datasets (TinyImageNet, LSUN, SVHN), indicating strong generalization of the Mahalanobis-based detector.

steps.heading: Adversarial Robustness
steps.content: Performance drops slightly on DeepFool samples but remains competitive, showing resilience against adversarial attacks.

steps.heading: Block-wise Analysis
steps.content: Scores vary by block index, suggesting some network layers are more reliable for confidence estimation.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: The method provides a solid baseline for confidence scoring, performing well even under challenging conditions.

slides.title: Algorithmic Framework
slides.notes: Describes the core algorithms for computing confidence scores and updating classifiers incrementally.

steps.heading: Algorithm 1: Confidence Score
steps.content: Computes a confidence score by aggregating Mahalanobis distances across network layers, incorporating noise perturbation for robustness.

steps.heading: Algorithm 2: Incremental Learning
steps.content: Updates class mean and shared covariance upon encountering new classes, enabling seamless integration into lifelong learning systems.

steps.heading: Key Design
steps.content: Uses Gaussian distributions to model feature variability per layer, making the score sensitive to distribution shifts.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: These algorithms provide a principled way to quantify uncertainty and adapt to new data without retraining the entire model.

slides.title: Robustness and Generalization
slides.notes: Highlights the method's robustness under noisy or imbalanced training data, and its ability to generalize from simple attacks to complex ones.

steps.heading: Hyperparameter Robustness
steps.content: Performance remains stable even when hyperparameters are tuned using only in-distribution data, without needing OOD validation samples.

steps.heading: Noise and Imbalance Resilience
steps.content: The method performs well even with noisy labels or small training datasets, outperforming baseline methods in extreme scenarios.

steps.heading: Generalization to Complex Attacks
steps.content: A model trained on a simple attack (e.g., FGSM) can effectively detect more complex attacks (e.g., DeepFool, CW), demonstrating strong transferability.

steps.heading: Cross-Task Performance
steps.content: The framework excels in detecting both OOD samples and adversarial attacks, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both domains.

slides.title: Class-Incremental Learning Application
slides.notes: Demonstrates how the method enables seamless addition of new classes without retraining, supporting lifelong learning.

steps.heading: Incremental Class Addition
steps.content: When new classes are introduced, the method updates class means and shared covariance, enabling detection of out-of-distribution samples without retraining.

steps.heading: Performance Comparison
steps.content: Outperforms baseline methods (e.g., Euclidean distance and retrained softmax) in maintaining base class accuracy while adding new classes.

steps.heading: Memory Efficiency
steps.content: Requires only a small number of old samples to maintain performance, reducing memory overhead in real-world deployment.

steps.heading: Practical Implications
steps.content: Enables efficient deployment in dynamic environments where new classes are added over time, such as in medical or security applications.

slides.title: Conclusion and Future Work
slides.notes: Summarizes findings and suggests avenues for further research.

steps.heading: Main Contributions
steps.content: Proposed a novel Mahalanobis-distance-based framework for robust classification and incremental learning.

steps.heading: Experimental Validation
steps.content: Results show consistent improvement over baseline methods across multiple benchmarks and attack scenarios.

steps.heading: Limitations
steps.content: Performance can degrade with very noisy or highly imbalanced data; requires careful hyperparameter tuning.

steps.heading: Future Directions
steps.content: Plan to extend to other architectures and integrate with more advanced uncertainty quantification techniques.

steps.heading: Thank You
steps.content: Thank you for your attention!
